title: Reading Comprehension: Inference
prerequisites: distinguish what is stated from what is implied
learning_objectives: Choose the option that must be true given the passage - no outside knowledge.
Prefer small, well-supported inferences over large leaps.
Reject options that go beyond, distort, or contradict the passage.

pedagogical_model.sequence: I do -> we do -> you do

opening.builds_on: Telling a stated fact from an implied one.

items.prompt: An RC inference must be what, relative to the passage?
items.answer: Strongly supported / must be true given the text.

items.prompt: May you use outside knowledge on inference questions?
Are big, dramatic inferences usually right on the GMAT?
items.answer: No - the GMAT rewards small, safe inferences.

retrieval_starter.purpose: Recalls the strict 'supported by the passage only' bar for inference.
retrieval_starter.if_students_struggle: Have them point to the exact sentence that supports a candidate inference.

questions.prompt: How do you test a candidate inference?
questions.answer: Find the passage sentence(s) that force it.

questions.prompt: Why reject an inference that needs outside facts?
questions.answer: RC inferences must rest on the passage alone.

opening.prior_knowledge_bridge: You can tell stated from implied. An inference question asks for the implied claim the passage makes almost inevitable - the smallest safe step beyond the words, supported by a specific sentence, never a leap that needs outside facts.
opening.learning_intention: By the end you can select the choice the passage most supports and reject leaps beyond it.
success_criteria: I can point to the sentence that supports my inference.
I can prefer a small, safe inference over a dramatic one.
I can reject options that add outside information.

opening_script.move: Do Now on screen; learners answer from memory, no notes.
Cold-call answers; name the argument's conclusion vs premises.
Bridge to today's question type; state what the stem is really asking.
Move to I do: model the reasoning on one worked item.

i_do.stem:
Passage: The town's only bridge is closed for repairs until spring. During the closure, the ferry is the sole way to cross the river, and it does not run after dark.

Question: It can be inferred from the passage that, during the closure,

options.A: there is no way to cross the river after dark
options.C: the ferry is faster than the bridge was
options.D: most residents rarely need to cross the river
options.E: the ferry runs more often than it did before

i_do.explanation: The ferry is the SOLE crossing and doesn't run after dark, so during the closure there is no after-dark crossing. A is a small, forced inference; the others add facts the passage never gives.
think_aloud_steps: Facts: ferry is the only crossing; ferry stops after dark.
Combine: after dark, no crossing at all.
A is forced; B/C/D/E need outside information.
i_do.key_takeaway: Combine the passage's own statements into the smallest claim they force - nothing extra.

we_do.stem:
Passage: Every painting in the exhibit was completed after 1950. The museum's oldest painting was completed in 1600.

Question: The passage most strongly supports which of the following?

options.A: The museum's oldest painting is not in the exhibit.
options.B: The exhibit contains the museum's most valuable paintings.
options.D: The exhibit has more paintings than the rest of the museum.

we_do.explanation: Exhibit paintings are all post-1950; the 1600 painting is pre-1950, so it can't be in the exhibit. A is forced; the rest add unsupported claims.
scaffold_hints: Compare the dates: exhibit is post-1950; the oldest is 1600.
A pre-1950 painting cannot be in a post-1950 exhibit.
Reject options needing value, rarity, or founding date.

immediate_feedback.if_correct: Right - the date rule excludes the 1600 painting.
immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: Value and rarity aren't in the passage; only dates are.

we_do.stem:
Passage: The clinic accepts walk-in patients only on weekday mornings. On weekday afternoons and all weekend, patients must have an appointment.

Question: It can be inferred that a patient without an appointment

options.A: cannot be seen on a Saturday
options.B: will always be seen faster than one with an appointment
options.D: is never accepted by the clinic
options.E: must pay more than a patient with an appointment

we_do.explanation: Walk-ins are allowed only weekday mornings; weekends require an appointment, so a walk-in cannot be seen Saturday. A is forced; the others add unsupported facts.
scaffold_hints: When are walk-ins allowed? Only weekday mornings.
Saturday is a weekend, so an appointment is required.
Avoid options about speed or cost - not stated.

immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: D is too strong (walk-ins ARE accepted weekday mornings).

you_do.stem:
Passage: All of the company's electric vehicles were built at its newest plant. The company's first plant, built in 1980, produces only gasoline vehicles.

Question: The passage most strongly supports which of the following?

options.A: No electric vehicle was built at the 1980 plant.
options.B: The newest plant is larger than the 1980 plant.
options.C: Gasoline vehicles are cheaper to build.
options.D: The company stopped building gasoline vehicles.
options.E: The 1980 plant will be closed soon.

you_do.explanation: Electric vehicles all come from the newest plant, and the 1980 plant makes only gasoline vehicles, so no electric vehicle came from it. A is forced; the rest add facts.

you_do.stem:
Passage: The festival is held outdoors and is canceled whenever it rains. This year the festival was held as scheduled.

Question: It can be inferred from the passage that this year

options.A: it did not rain on the festival day
options.B: the festival was more popular than last year
options.C: the weather is usually sunny in the region
options.D: the festival will be held indoors next year

you_do.explanation: Rain forces cancellation; the festival was held, so by the contrapositive it did not rain. A is forced; the others are unsupported.

you_do.stem:
Passage: Researchers noted that a certain frog species is found only in caves that stay near a constant cool temperature year-round. A newly surveyed cave has temperatures that swing widely between seasons.

Question: The passage most strongly supports which of the following about the newly surveyed cave?

options.A: The frog species is unlikely to be found living there.
options.B: The cave is larger than the frogs' usual caves.
options.C: The frogs would thrive there if introduced.
options.D: The cave has no other animal species.
options.E: The temperature swings are caused by the frogs.

you_do.explanation: The frog needs constant cool temperatures; this cave swings widely, so the frog is unlikely to live there. A is the supported inference; the others add or reverse facts.

mastery_check.definition: A topic is mastered at >=85% correct on new/held-out-style questions across >=2 spaced sessions with delay (PRD Section 9.2). Flashcard recall alone is not mastery; application under mixed, delayed conditions is the bar.
conditions: >=85% correct on new/held-out-style items (not flashcard recall) per session
sustained across >=2 spaced sessions with a delay between them
per-topic memory surfaced only after >=20 graded reviews (PRD give-up rule)
per-topic performance surfaced only after >=8 application attempts (PRD give-up rule)
mastery_check.on_fail: Not yet: route every miss to the required error log (tag why: content gap / reasoning flaw / timing / careless / trap), schedule spaced re-practice via topic-aware scheduling, then re-enter the we-do -> you-do loop before re-testing.
